instructional_goal: Given a cross-functional product discussion or stakeholder inquiry, you will apply FaceStation 2 technical specifications — including matching performance, user capacity, illuminance range, credential options, interface capabilities, and model variant differences — to accurately answer product questions and justify product decisions without referencing external support.
audience: Hardware Product Owners and internal product stakeholders responsible for the Suprema FaceStation 2 biometric access control terminal
conditions: During cross-functional meetings (engineering, sales, support, or leadership) where FaceStation 2 capabilities are questioned or compared
When responding to internal or customer-facing product inquiries that require accurate specification recall or trade-off explanation
When making or defending product prioritization decisions based on hardware capabilities and constraints
tools_and_resources: Suprema FaceStation 2 product datasheet and specification table
Quick-reference job aid summarizing key specs and model variant differences (FS2-D vs. FS2-AWB)

in_scope: Key performance specifications: matching speed, user capacity, and operating illuminance range
Credential and card compatibility: supported RF card types, frequencies, and read ranges by model variant
Hardware interfaces and connectivity options: Ethernet, Wi-Fi, RS-485, Wiegand, USB, Relay, TTL
Model variant differentiation: FS2-D vs. FS2-AWB feature and credential differences
Security features: live face detection, anti-spoofing, Android 5.0 security layer
System configuration options: standalone, standalone-secure, and distributed access control setups
out_of_scope: Physical installation, mounting, or wiring procedures
BioStar 2 software administration, user enrollment workflows, or firmware management
Troubleshooting, diagnostics, or device repair procedures
Competitive benchmarking or pricing and commercial terms

assumptions: Alex has foundational familiarity with biometric access control concepts (e.g., 1:1 vs. 1:N matching, RFID card types) but has not yet mastered FaceStation 2-specific specifications
Alex regularly participates in cross-functional discussions where accurate product specification knowledge is expected without look-up delays
Alex does not require hands-on device operation skills — the performance need is decisional and communicative, not technical-operational
The 30-minute course will be supplemented by a leave-behind job aid or reference document for ongoing use post-training
Alex has access to the FaceStation 2 datasheet as a reference resource but needs structured instruction to internalize and apply the most decision-critical specifications

evidence.relevance: Defines ultra-performance specifications (matching speed 1:4,000/sec, user capacity 30,000/4,000) that Alex must be able to cite and apply in product discussions
Covers the operating illuminance range (0–25,000 lx), a key differentiating capability Alex must articulate to stakeholders evaluating deployment environments
Describes multi-RFID card reading capabilities across dual frequency bands, directly relevant to credential compatibility questions from sales and engineering
Full specification table covering both FS2-D and FS2-AWB model variants across all categories — the primary reference for model differentiation decisions
Details enhanced security features (live face detection, anti-spoofing) that Alex must be able to explain when justifying security-related product decisions
System configuration diagram illustrating standalone, standalone-secure, and distributed deployment scenarios — essential for Alex to match product capabilities to customer use cases
Ergonomics and usability features (height range, tilt bracket, GUI) that Alex must reference when addressing accessibility or usability requirements from diverse stakeholders

confidence_rationale: The technical documentation provides comprehensive, well-structured specification data directly aligned with the persona's stated needs. The persona's pain points (stakeholder alignment, translating specs into decisions) map clearly to a performance-oriented goal around applying specifications. The 30-minute duration is realistic for a focused specification-mastery goal scoped to a single product's datasheet content. The only uncertainty is whether the course should also address BioStar 2 integration depth, which the SME should clarify.
questions_for_sme: Should the course address how FaceStation 2 integrates with BioStar 2 software at a conceptual level, or is that covered in a separate training module?

Are there specific stakeholder scenarios (e.g., a sales objection, an engineering trade-off, a support escalation) that Alex most frequently encounters and that should anchor the instructional examples?
Is the FS2-D vs. FS2-AWB model differentiation a frequent source of confusion or misalignment internally, and should it receive disproportionate instructional emphasis?
Are there any specifications in the datasheet that are commonly misquoted or misunderstood by internal teams — areas where misconceptions need to be explicitly corrected?
Will learners have access to the FaceStation 2 datasheet as a job aid during and after the course, or is the expectation that they recall specifications from memory in real-world situations?
